speaker: interviewer
text: There's several things we need to discuss on this reel. The first being how you went to Hillcrest Country Club and got money. How did you-- how did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had as-- I had as members of the community relations committee, some of the most influential and successful leaders of the Jewish community, many from the motion picture industry. And some of these people also belonged to Hillcrest Country Club. So if I feel this person or that person is ripe for me to take advantage of him and of his time, I said, it's about time you take me to Hillcrest. And he knew what I wanted. I told him what I wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would take you to Hillcrest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I would have the right to mention their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these were members of the Jewish community. They were, many of them, members of the film industry. All-- all of them rather influential, and who could afford to belong to Hillcrest. And he would take me-- they-- any one of them would take me into the card room. And there they were playing cards, and for rather high stakes. He would take me-- most of those people knew me. Oh, this is Joe Roos, who knows all about the Nazis. You know, there was very [INAUDIBLE] Jews. And so he would take me from one table to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes, this man sitting here would push-- if this is not enough. Give more, you know. And so I wound up with quite a pocket full of money. And then I sent it to my sister. And my sister was-- some of the money that I got-- where have I got that Berichte now? Some of the money that I got paid for this. And what are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are called Deutschland-Berichte. And this one is called Germany. So Deutschland-Berichte, obviously, is in German-- is German-- and this one is in English. And it tells what's going on in Germany and so on and so forth. If I were to-- I would have too much trouble looking at table of contents with a magnifying glass in order to tell you what's inside. So I have to be satisfied with that. But one of the things which financed my operation-- no, that's not the right-- one of the things-- some of the money I collected was used to publish the first book in German, different chapters written by different people, about their experiences in the concentration camp. Two of the writers later became acquaintances of mine. One of them started publishing a weekly German newspaper in Chicago. And then the other one was a member of the Reichstag, which is-- was the German house of Parliament, which Mr. Hitler burned down in order to get more votes in the then upcoming election. Because he did not have control of the Parliament. He needed a few more votes. So one of the things they did is they burned the Reichstag and said, this is what the Communists did. So vote anti-communists. Vote for us. Now that book I got from my sister. And when Spielberg received an honorary degree from the University of Southern California, for which I worked, I gave that book to somebody to give to the president of the university, who presented it as a memo to Spielberg. And I included in it a few of the typical pro-Nazi anti-Semitic leaflets, so that he would have an idea of what-- what is behind it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about the surveillance in Long Beach? How you managed the surveillance in Long Beach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what you mean by-- by surveillance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you managed to detect or uncover any kind of Nazi infiltration in Long Beach, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, through the same man that I mentioned to you earlier, who gave me the Ku Klux Klan study. He was real active there. But the heavy-- the heavy activity was down at 634 West 15th Street. No more than that. Here and there, a little bit of attempts to find out things which sometimes were valueless and sometimes valuable-- about anything pertaining to our Navy-- but much more down in San Diego than in Long Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what in San Diego?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what in San Diego?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the Nazis they had down there were hanging around the-- the bars a great deal-- a very great deal. What was called bars at that time. That was-- that was just about all. I-- I-- I do not consider that was of-- of any significance.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How about a man named Ness who was an infiltrator?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ness was one of my men. How did you know his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a little birdie told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- his-- his name was N-2.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I originally met him in Chicago. He was trying to be an architect, but he was not much of an architect. His life was wrapped up in publishing a liberal magazine. And how I got to know him, I don't remember anymore. But he found me. And I don't know how it came about. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he did some undercover work for me over there. And one fine day, he-- he blew into town. And I had him-- I had him cover the German house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Once he was set up to make a speech at the German house. They said he's got to talk in English. He could-- could not speak German. He understood a little bit. And so he says, Joe, what am I going to say? So I gave him an outline for a speech in English. I wanted to see for myself how he does that. And that was before the-- the Anschluss, you know? And so I took Avina along. I didn't tell her about my knowing this guy or any relationship. And I wanted her reaction to it. I said, what do you think? Well, she was so angry. [LAUGHS] That-- That dirty so and so. He did a good job.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never, never tried to talk them into doing any violence or any-- I didn't preach hate. I preached the wonderful [GERMAN], or the great-- the great good, the great Schiller [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, he knew-- I knew how to rattle him. I left out the name of Heine. Because Heine-- I mean, the-- the three great ones were Goethe, Schiller, Heine. Heine was Jewish. Therefore, his most famous poem that every child had to learn was eliminated from every book and printed again with the bottom saying, author unknown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to recite it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the whole thing. [GERMAN] Something-- it's sad. I-- I cannot tell-- I cannot tell what it really means, but I can't get it out of my head. [GERMAN] Lorelei is the name of the poem. Lorelei is a woman that lived under the water. It tells the story and it is the hallelujah for the Rhine. You know, we worshipped the Rhine like it's something extraordinary. The Mississippi is nothing in comparison.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have any-- do you still have any of the speeches that you wrote that anyone delivered? For instance, like this man Ness?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of information was this man Ness able to give to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, hang around, find out, engage in conversation. Lots of talk, lots of talk, some of it meaningless really. Seeing what the others were doing. You see, this-- these people were busy convincing each other that they were right in admiring Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about any Hitler Youth here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you had the Hitler Youth here. I wish we weren't so tied up here. I would go and look for some of the-- some of the news research service issues, where I show what they're doing with the Hitler Youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you-- can you describe anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, they just trained them to-- to-- to agree with their parents. That's all. I mean, march an awful lot, sing German song, some of the stormtrooper songs and so on and so forth.

words.text: and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had little uniforms with swastikas, if not here, then there, you know. It didn't-- it didn't do too much good. I don't think it really went over with the youngsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the dumber ones went for it. I mean, those were-- were the typical bright American kids. They didn't go for it much.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point while you were doing all this work-- this is starting and continuing through World War II-- did you ever witness any large parades, what-- Nazi rally parades?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not witness any of them. I have here one picture that shows the [INAUDIBLE] guys carrying a coffin. And on the coffin was written, Versailles-- Versailles, meaning the Versailles treaty, which understandably-- even understandable to me-- is the-- was or is the fact that the Versailles Treaty was a rotten no-good document. Clemenceau rammed it down the throat of the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, was there someone named, I think is it, Ferenz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ferenz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ferenz.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was FK Ferenz. F-E-R-E-N-Z. He ran German movies. He wrote a book about-- I have it in there-- about the great things you should know about Nazi Germany. And there were some [NON-ENGLISH] working for some of the universities, who thought the book was excellent from their standpoint. Ferenz was prosecuted, along with six others, by Warren-- Earl-- by Earl Warren, when Warren was Attorney General, and then became Governor, and then became Supreme Court Justice-- Chief Justice. Ferenz was prosecuted for failure-- he and several others-- for failure to register as a foreign agent under the California law in Sacramento. And I was the technical advisor, hiding in a little room behind the court.

speaker: interviewer
text: When something came up, the prosecutor would say, well, what does this mean? And how-- how do I do this? So I traveled back and forth. When court was on, I was up there. And on Saturday and Sunday, I was working down here. During the-- when we had to change the tape, there was something you started to talk about, which I really think is important. And that is how you were able to give to newspapermen, like Drew Pearson, information.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the most important outlet was the News Research Service. I had some friends on the newspapers. I had one friend in the City News Service. So when I wanted to plant something, I usually used them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I worked hard to build good relations to the major newspapers. And I did something that you couldn't do nowadays. Now when you go down to the Times, you have to have a good reason before the guy lets you go upstairs. Who do you want to see and why? You know, that kind of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would deliberately go down with a story. We didn't need to worry about dating, and go right up to the city desk, sit down at the typewriter-- say hello to the guys-- sit down at the typewriter, and knock out a story. Or, if there was some public event and I would see there's a guy from there's the guy from Manchester [INAUDIBLE] paper, And nobody here from such and such paper. I would call them up and say, look I'm here. Such and such is going on. So on so is here from the or whatever. And nobody from the paper there. And I would give them a story right over the telephone. So I made-- I worked at making myself one of the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the information that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I also had people down there that would tip me off of things, too. Yeah, I don't know whether the name Herb Brin means anything to you. Herb Brin publishes which is an Anglo-Jewish newspaper. He was my tipster down at the If something came up at the wire that came his way, he'd say, hey such and such is having-- better get-- get going. Do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what kind of things would come over the wire that would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, any-- anything that would have a Jewish angle. Like let us assume-- let us assume there was two alleged rabbis who are now in trouble. This would have happened at-- while-- while Brin was on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, look, two rabbis are-- can you do something? So then I would figure out, what do we do? Do I get the board of rabbis to go and ban them, you know, like they did in the olden days? You know, what? Well, at least he would tip me off. Can I do something? I would do it. I'd get the board of rabbis to issue a statement or what. Like, it depends on what I find out and what is there. It's-- my mother used to say, I'm going into the [GERMAN]-- this is where you can kept your food-- to see what we're going to eat tonight. What do you think, possibly, is the most important-- of all the-- of all the things that you did at that time-- what do you think is the most important to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it's-- it's in two different ways. One, as I've said before, is the News Research Service, because my feeling was, because having been in newspapers, make the world know in this bound volume-- in this bound volume of that one newspaper that we published in Chicago-- the dedication, say something-- to awaken the public, you know. And that was after-- immediately after Hitler came to power. So this was important to me, and I think it is important from the-- your standpoint. The other thing is, what I would describe as channeling the race relations field. I created what became known as the Community Relations Conference of Southern California, which was composed of almost 100 member organization-- many of them labor unions, many of them churches. Individual churches and some of them whole denomination. For instance, when the riots took place in Watts, it must have been 10, 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: 30 Years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 Years ago. When the riots took place, the Council of Churches of Southern California created an-- a-- an group-- created a body, a committee-- Commission on Church and Race. And what do you think? I was a member of it. And every time I came to that meeting, I would say, here comes the honorary Aryan. I mean, they wanted help. So we created the-- we created this thing. And that was useful in many fights, where the fight had to be fought, not on behalf of the Jews, but on behalf of all of those underprivileged, especially blacks and, what we then called Mexicans, now we call Hispanics. That organization died about four years ago. All of its papers and all of its documents are now in the School of-- School of Social Work at USC. Lot of good files. Lot of good files. They also had a-- a sister organization, which was called Community Relations Education Foundation, because we were active politically. We were active politically. We could be active politically. So if we could get a good-- a sizable amount of money from somebody, we would put it where we are out of politics. This one for, this-- so we used it for-- we defied-- we defeated Jack Tenney. Jack-- Senator Jack Tenney, who then represented the entire county of Los Angeles up in Sacramento as a Senator, was the head of the un-American Activities Committee. And he swallowed the-- the protocols of the elders of Zion and became a running mate for-- for Gerald LK Smith. He-- Smith for Vice President, he Tenney for-- for for-- he ran for President, and Tenney for Vice President. And we defeated him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But 'we' does not mean now the Jewish community. We couldn't touch politics, because of the nations, right? We did this over there. And then we had some people sitting on the school board who were [INAUDIBLE], who did not permit teaches to talk about the United Nations, about Eleanor Roosevelt, about the human-- the declaration of human rights, which was a product of that communist Eleanor Roosevelt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And encouraged kids to tell the management in our schools that teacher so and so talked nicely about the United Nations or Eleanor Roosevelt. We defeated all of them, but not as the Jewish community. I mean, this was separate, because we took our heads off and put different heads on. This was some of the important things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once Pearl Harbor was bombed, and we got--

speaker: interviewee
text: What? What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Once Pearl Harbor was bombed and the United States got into the war, how much more of your work was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well practically the anti-Nazi work was finished, but then the race relations work was increased. And I think we accomplished more, far more, than the NAACP and the Urban League, and a couple of others like that. First of all, we had more money. Secondly, there was too much conflict between the individuals and between groups. And they were really responsible for the destruction of the organization about four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years, and the war is over-- and basically I think you've been talking about this all along-- but is there something that you would like to pass on to people in the future, who will look at this tape, about what you learned, having grown up in Germany and then coming to the United States and having witnessed fascism firsthand? What is it you'd like to pass on about what you've learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll first tell you something that I did, and then you will understand what-- what my answer is. I felt that the average American knows far too little about his own country. Knows far too little about the early days, the Founders, the Constitution, the Bill of Rights-- far too little. Oh, they read somewhere something about Bill of Rights. Who was Jefferson, who was Monroe and some of the other people? And because of that, I did a daily radio program on KFWB at 8:00, from 8:00 to 8:30 in the evening, without any commercials. And the whole thing was called, For This We Fight. And after the war was over, is For This We Fought. And every night it was something different. United Nations, as you could see from some of this, was always under attack. You know, United Nations was a communist device, according to them. Monday it was United Nations, the people's hope. The second one was something about the Constitution. No, the second one was the Constitution-- yeah, the Bill of Rights and the Constitution. On Monday, we had different organizations-- primarily women's organizations-- come on for interviews. This one is interested in health so it dealt with United Nations section on health. This is not the correct name for it. I forget it. If it was labor, obviously the labor guys came on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on Monday, always dealing with one United Nations facet, especially UNESCO. But they cannot come on the air unless every member of their organization-- let us assume it's the League of University Women-- every member of the League of University Women has to-- has to be reached. Saying listen in on KFWB on such and such time, blah, blah, blah. That's the way I built an audience, that I had higher rating for the station than any of the so-called network stations. The local stuff [INAUDIBLE]. So one of them was The Atomic Age is Here. And this was preaching how to-- how to understand what is happening on-- on the international scene. The Atomic Age is Here. You know who Leo Szilard was? Leo Szilard who took Einstein to Roosevelt to explain-- I interviewed Leo Szilard.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I wrote the whole interview and I had a guy with a good voice, without a German accent, do all of the shows. Most of them didn't know Joe Roos existed. Leo Szilard talked me into doing a show once a week, The Atomic Age is Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got to tell you a funny one. There was a fellow in Pasadena who was an [INAUDIBLE]. This guy called and said, what do I know about the atomics? So he put me together with a man who-- whose name I can't remember-- was an expert at it. He was always a very well-known professor. He helped me. He got me ones who-- I shouldn't say I did-- I arranged the interview with the fellow. So he came there at the right time. And I interviewed him. And then I wrote out the series of questions, for a-- a guy who really was no more than an announcer. And when he was finished-- well, he was driven there, I should say-- when he was finished, I took him to his car.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said goodbye to him, while thanking him. The driver left the key inside. Here's a guy who could blow up the whole world. And you should have seen what problems they had to get back into the car. This is one thing about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But wait, I-- we're running out of time on our tape. And what I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me finish one. It's too good too-- not to. I asked him, do you think the Russians know how to build an atomic bomb? I said-- after he was off the air. He says, yes. We have five ways of doing it. I'm quite sure the Russians have six. I'll never forget in all my life. [LAUGHING] And then there was some others. Friday was a catchall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So getting back to my question, is there something that you would like to pass on to people in the future who look at this tape, who-- what you've learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that I would-- let me put it differently. If I could live my life all over again and not do what I did do, I would want to become a teacher. I would want to become a fighter for better education. Education-- not only how to do-- do math. I mean we are doing all right technically. But an all-around education, in order to become mensch, decent human beings. That's what I would like to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We just have a moment or so left on the tape. And I thought maybe you might want to introduce your wife to us. Would you like to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get her. You can just sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I-- am I tied up?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Roos, I see you have someone sitting here beside you. Would you like to introduce me-- us to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like everyone to know my good wife, Avina. It'll be 60 years this December that we are-- we are married. She is a grandmother, is very proud of her grandchildren and of her son and daughter-in-law. I told you earlier that she was a nurse before I married her. And she's been nursing me all this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you both, [INTERPOSING VOICES] for you allowing us into your home, for you spending so much time with us, Mr. Roos. And I think that this tape will be seen and heard by many, many people. I can't thank you enough for all the time and effort you've given to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was our pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you, and we wish you good luck. And I hope many people see this and hopefully learn something, in order to build a better world, which we need so desperately.
